definition: A directional bias (bullish or bearish) established through market structure analysis on higher timeframes (daily/weekly). It is determined by price breaking or failing to break prior highs/lows, liquidity sweeps, Fair Value Gap interactions, and CISD patterns. Bias forms the top layer of Top Down Analysis, guiding the trader to only take setups in the bias direction.
identification_rules: Mark previous day high (PDH) and previous day low (PDL); if price closes above PDH, bias is bullish; if below PDL, bearish. Same logic for previous week high/low.
If price touches PDH/PDL but fails to close beyond (Failure to Displace), bias reverses: failure above PDH → bearish, failure below PDL → bullish.

On higher timeframe, identify Internal Range Liquidity (FVG) and External Range Liquidity (swing high/low). After price sweeps external liquidity, expect movement toward internal liquidity; after price respects FVG, expect movement toward external liquidity.
If price crosses through a Fair Value Gap instead of respecting it, reassess bias.

On daily chart, identify High Resistance Liquidity (already swept swing points) and Low Resistance Liquidity (unswept swing points, i.e., Failure Swings). If High Resistance Liquidity is below price and Low Resistance Liquidity above, bias is bullish; reverse for bearish.
Observe higher timeframe candles for CISD (close inside previous candle's range) to confirm bias direction.

If previous higher timeframe candle is a bearish reversal candle (open high close low, wick larger than body), bias is bearish; if bullish reversal (open low close high, wick larger than body), bias is bullish.

If price sweeps prior low and closes back inside range (e.g., 0.5 wick respected), bias is bullish; if price sweeps prior high then forms bearish engulfing and closes below prior low, bias is bearish.
If direction is unclear (neutral), wait for which side is closed beyond first to determine bias.
Once bias is set, maintain it until Draw On Liquidity is reached; if price fails to close beyond key structure, maintain original bias.
Observe swing highs/lows and price displacement: if price fails to break a swing point and returns inside range, bias is toward the opposite direction.
After establishing bias, only trade displacement and failure breakout patterns in the bias direction.
trading_implication: Bias is the top layer of Top Down Analysis (Bias → Structure → Entry). After determining bias, only take trades in that direction. For bullish bias, look for bullish displacement and bullish failure breakouts; for bearish bias, look for bearish displacement and bearish failure breakouts. Use higher timeframe bias to filter lower timeframe entries via FVG or Order Block. Target Low Resistance Liquidity (Draw On Liquidity) and place stop beyond High Resistance Liquidity. Higher timeframe bias lasts longer.
common_mistakes: Establishing bias when market structure is unclear, then stubbornly trading against the actual direction.
Ignoring displacement confirmation and judging bias based on a single swing point.
Not updating bias when price breaks a swing point, maintaining original bias.
Holding bias when price touches PDH/PDL but fails to close beyond, ignoring Failure to Displace.
Forcing a bias when price is ranging and no clear breakout occurs, instead of waiting for a close beyond one side.
Switching bias prematurely before Draw On Liquidity is reached.
Holding bias when price crosses through FVG instead of respecting it, without reassessing.
Judging bias on a single timeframe without considering higher timeframe internal/external liquidity relationships.
After price moves from external to internal liquidity, entering reverse without waiting for structure confirmation (e.g., CISD).
Mistaking unswept swing points as High Resistance Liquidity (price must close through the level to confirm).
Ignoring whether PDH/PDL have been swept, judging bias solely on current price without High/Low Resistance Liquidity distribution.
Mistaking lower timeframe counter-bias moves as trend reversals when they may just form wicks on higher timeframe candles.
Not confirming bias with higher timeframe candle patterns, entering based solely on lower timeframe signals.
Taking setups opposite to higher timeframe bias (e.g., bullish setups when bias is bearish).
merge_notes: Both projects are ICT school; no school conflict. The Foundation card provides more detailed identification rules and trading implications, while Education - ICT card adds simpler displacement-based rules. Unified card preserves all rules from both, deduplicating where identical (e.g., failure to break swing point).
